text: The surgeon marks the stoma site, noting it is too medial to the wound, and adjusts the position more laterally along the line within the rectus muscle. An incision is made away from the midline, and a closed suction drain is inserted into the pelvis.

The surgeon inserts a lap sponge into the abdomen and holds it against the abdominal wall at the intended stoma site. Using a #15 blade, a two-centimeter circular section of skin is excised, ensuring it is positioned far enough from the pravena system to prevent enteric content spillage.

The surgeon dissects the subcutaneous tissues vertically with electrocautery down to the fascia, which is then incised superiorly and inferiorly. A Kelly clamp is inserted through the muscle to the posterior fascia, spreading the fibers bluntly, and the posterior fascia is incised. The Kelly clamp grasps a lap sponge to protect the bowel. The fascial opening is approximately two centimeters in diameter. A lap sponge is placed through the defect for hemostasis. The selected bowel section is marked with a brown chromic tag on the distal end and a blue vicryl on the proximal end for orientation. A tonsil clamp penetrates the mesentery, and moist umbilical tape is used to pull the loop through the fascial defect.

The surgeon places the stoma rod after closing the midline incision and applying the Pravena system. Interrupted figure-of-eight sutures using O-sized monofilament absorbable sections, such as Maxon or PDS, are used to close the midline. The sutures are snapped and organized on a long clamp for proper order.

The surgeon closes the skin using surgical staples, cleans and dries the area around the incision. A sponge is placed over the ostomy to provide a dry surface for the Pravena dressing. Benzoin is applied around the wound border to aid adherence. The Pravena system is positioned to fully cover the wound, and its backing is removed before placing the dressing over the incision.

The surgeon presses down firmly on the hypercolloid ring to ensure a seal, using the blunt end of an instrument around the sponge. The backing on the side sections of the dressing is removed, and a slit is cut out for the drain if necessary.
